summary: SKTouchEventArgs.Pressure is always 1 on Windows (UWP/WinUI) and iOS when using a pen or stylus, because the Windows and Apple platform touch handlers do not read pressure data from the underlying pointer/touch APIs — only the Android handler correctly passes pressure.

bugSignals.errorMessage: Pressure is always 1 even when an actual pen is used

stepsToReproduce: Create a SKCanvasView with EnableTouchEvents=true in a UWP or MAUI Windows app
Use a Surface Pen or Wacom stylus
Read args.Pressure — it is always 1.0 regardless of applied pressure
reproEvidence.environmentDetails: SkiaSharp 2.80.3, UWP 19041, MS Surface; also confirmed on iOS and MAUI with Wacom tablet (.NET 10)

repoLinks.description: Reporter's WinAPI workaround using GetPointerPenInfo P/Invoke
Reporter confirms iOS also doesn't set pressure (UITouch.Force not used); notes MAUI variant does set it
2025 confirmation: Android works, Windows (Wacom tablet, .NET 10 MAUI) still pressure=1

versionAnalysis.relevanceReason: Code investigation confirms Windows and Apple MAUI handlers still use constructors defaulting pressure to 1; Android handler was separately fixed and works.

analysis.summary: The Windows (MAUI/WinUI) and Apple (iOS/MAUI) SKTouchHandler implementations omit pressure when constructing SKTouchEventArgs, causing Pressure to always default to 1. The Windows handler should read pointerPoint.Properties.Pressure; the Apple handler should read UITouch.Force / UITouch.MaximumPossibleForce. The Android handler correctly reads evt.GetPressure(pointer) and works as expected.
analysis.rationale: Source code inspection of all platform SKTouchHandler files confirms the root cause. Windows CommonHandler calls SKTouchEventArgs(id, touchActionType, mouse, device, skPoint, isInContact, wheelDelta) — the 7-argument constructor that defaults pressure to 1. Apple FireEvent calls SKTouchEventArgs(id, actionType, point, inContact) — the 4-argument overload that also defaults to 1. Android passes evt.GetPressure(pointer) as the 8th argument and works correctly. This is a clear missing feature/implementation bug on Windows and iOS.

keySignals.text: The pressure is always at 1 even when an actual pen is used and even recognised as SKTouchDeviceType.Pen
keySignals.interpretation: Pen device type detection works; only pressure is missing — confirms the platform handler reads device type but skips pressure.

keySignals.text: pressure is not even used in the iOS implementation, so it never gets even set
keySignals.interpretation: Reporter independently discovered the same code gap in Apple handler — UITouch.Force is unused.

keySignals.text: Pressure works as intended on my Android device (Samsung Fold 5 with S Pen). Pressure does not work at all in Windows using a Wacom tablet
keySignals.interpretation: Confirms the Android fix is working; Windows bug persists into .NET 10 / MAUI era.

codeInvestigation.finding: CommonHandler constructs SKTouchEventArgs with 7-arg constructor (no pressure argument), defaulting pressure to 1. pointerPoint.Properties.Pressure from WinUI/WinRT PointerPointProperties is never read.
FireEvent constructs SKTouchEventArgs with 4-arg constructor, defaulting pressure to 1. UITouch.Force and UITouch.MaximumPossibleForce are never read.
Android correctly reads evt.GetPressure(pointer) and passes it as the 8th argument to the full SKTouchEventArgs constructor.
The 4-arg and 7-arg constructors both hardcode pressure=1 via this(…, 1). The 8-arg constructor accepts real pressure. Android uses 8-arg; Windows and Apple use shorter overloads.

workarounds: On Windows: P/Invoke GetPointerPenInfo from user32.dll using args.Id as pointerId to read real pressure (reported by original reporter — works but suboptimal perf)
On iOS: No known pure-managed workaround; UITouch.Force requires subclassing the gesture recognizer
nextQuestions: Does macOS (Pencil/tablet input) also fail? Tizen also uses the pressure-defaulting constructor.
Should the fix also cover the Tizen handler?
For iOS, should pressure only be set when UITouch.Type == UITouchType.Pencil to avoid returning Force for finger touches?

resolution.hypothesis: The fix requires two platform-level changes: (1) Windows handler reads pointerPoint.Properties.Pressure; (2) Apple handler reads touch.Force / touch.MaximumPossibleForce (guarded for Pencil type).

proposals.title: Read pressure in Windows handler
proposals.description: In CommonHandler, read pointerPoint.Properties.Pressure and pass it to the SKTouchEventArgs constructor.

proposals.title: Read pressure in Apple/iOS handler
proposals.description: In FireEvent, compute pressure as (float)(touch.Force / touch.MaximumPossibleForce) when MaximumPossibleForce > 0, else default to 1. Pass to SKTouchEventArgs.

proposals.title: WinAPI workaround (Windows only)
proposals.description: Use P/Invoke GetPointerPenInfo from user32.dll with the pointer ID from SKTouchEventArgs.Id to read pen pressure as a stop-gap.

resolution.recommendedReason: Windows is the most-affected platform per comments; fix is a one-liner reading pointerPoint.Properties.Pressure already available in scope.

actionability.reason: Root cause is clear from code inspection: Windows and Apple handlers omit pressure from SKTouchEventArgs. Both platform APIs expose pressure natively. Fix path is straightforward for both platforms.

actions.description: Apply bug, views-maui, windows, ios labels
Post triage findings and confirm root cause in code
actions.comment:
Thanks for the detailed report and animated screenshots!

After investigating the source, the root cause is confirmed: the Windows and iOS `SKTouchHandler` implementations do not read pressure from the underlying platform APIs when constructing `SKTouchEventArgs`, so `Pressure` always defaults to `1`.

- **Windows** (`Platform/Windows/SKTouchHandler.cs`): `pointerPoint.Properties.Pressure` is available from WinUI's `PointerPointProperties` but is never read.
- **iOS** (`Platform/Apple/SKTouchHandler.cs`): `UITouch.Force / UITouch.MaximumPossibleForce` is available for Apple Pencil input but is never read.
- **Android** works correctly — it already reads `evt.GetPressure(pointer)`.

This is a straightforward fix for both platforms. As a workaround on Windows, you can P/Invoke `GetPointerPenInfo` from `user32.dll` using `args.Id` as the pointer ID (as you discovered).

A fix PR adding pressure reads to both the Windows and Apple handlers would be welcome!
